questions.prompt: What is ApisCP, in one sentence?
choices: A cloud platform that hosts customer websites on a multi-tenant SaaS service operated by Apis Networks
A self-hosted multi-tenant hosting control panel an MSP installs on its own Linux servers
A reseller billing system that wraps cPanel and Plesk to standardise account creation
An RMM agent that monitors customer websites and sends uptime alerts
questions.explanation: ApisCP is the panel the MSP installs and operates on its own servers. There is no Apis Networks-managed service; the MSP runs the platform end-to-end.

questions.prompt: ApisCP charges a recurring per-account fee, similar to cPanel/WHM's per-account licensing tiers.
questions.explanation: ApisCP licenses per server with a one-time purchase. Per-account costs (storage, bandwidth, support time) are a function of the MSP's hosting plan, not the panel licence.

questions.prompt: Which of these are things the admin does in Nexus, not in the customer-facing panel? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Create a new customer account
Add a mailbox to an existing account
Suspend a customer account
Launch a Login-As session into a customer's account
Install WordPress on a customer's domain
Delete a customer account
questions.explanation: Create, suspend, delete, and Login-As are all Nexus actions. Adding a mailbox and installing WordPress are done inside the customer's account (either by the customer or by the admin after Login-As).

choices: Resets the customer's password to a temporary value so the admin can log in
Creates an authenticated session as the customer's admin user without a password handoff
Mirrors the customer's session into a read-only admin view
Sends the admin a one-time password the customer has to approve
questions.explanation: Login-As is server-side impersonation via `admin:hijack`. The admin gets a real session as the customer's admin user. No password is exposed or changed.

questions.prompt: The most common Login-As mistake is forgetting to click Revert Login-As before opening the next customer's account.
questions.explanation: Without Revert, the impersonated session carries into the next admin action and you end up acting as the wrong customer. Always Revert before moving on.

questions.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for adding a new mailbox under an existing account.
items: Open Users > Create User and fill in username, full name, password, storage limit
Under Add a New Address, type the local part and pick the domain
Set Type to Single User and assign the new user as the destination
Click Add Address and send a test message
questions.explanation: ApisCP separates users from addresses. Create the user first so they exist as a delivery destination, then create the address that delivers to them.

questions.prompt: A customer wants their second registered domain hosted in the same ApisCP account. Which surface is correct?
choices: Web > Subdomains, with the new domain typed as a single-word subdomain
DNS > Addon Domains, with the new domain typed as an FQDN
Account > Settings > Add Domain
A new Nexus account, since each domain needs its own account
questions.explanation: Addon Domains is the surface for a *different* registered domain inside the same account. Subdomains are names beneath an existing domain. A new account is wrong because addon domains share the account's resources and login.

questions.prompt: Which of these are first-look triage signals the lesson lists for an ApisCP ticket? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The Event ID on the customer's error page
The Job Runner queue state for the customer's pending action
The Storage Usage breakdown for the account
The 3-5-10 brute-force lockout when a customer can't log in
Running tcpdump on the server's mail port
Restarting the entire ApisCP service
questions.explanation: Event ID, Job Runner, Storage Usage, and the 3-5-10 lockout cover most walk-up tickets. Tcpdump and full service restarts are deep-end moves that come after these signals point you somewhere specific.

questions.prompt: A customer complains they can't log in from their office, but a colleague at the same office is fine. What's the most likely cause?
choices: The server has rejected the customer's IP from a different subnet
The customer triggered the 3-5-10 brute-force lockout by mistyping their password three times in five minutes
Their account has been suspended and the colleague's hasn't
Two-factor authentication is required and the customer hasn't enrolled
questions.explanation: Same office (same IP) implies a per-IP block, and Rampart's default 3-5-10 deterrent fires after three failures in a five-minute window. Wait ten minutes or `cpcmd rampart:unban <ip> '*'` from admin.

questions.prompt: When a customer is over storage quota, deleting files from /var/lib/mysql is a safe way to reclaim space.
questions.explanation: /var/lib/mysql contains live database files. Direct deletion corrupts the database. Drop databases from MySQL Manager instead. Same rule for /var/lib/pgsql (PostgreSQL) and /var/log/httpd (audit logs).

questions.prompt: Why is the Event ID the most important piece of information on an ApisCP error page?
choices: It tells the customer which web app caused the error
It is the unique lookup key in the admin's panel log; grepping for it surfaces the full stack trace
It identifies which platform release is running
It is the support ticket reference the customer should quote
questions.explanation: Without the Event ID, you hunt through logs by timestamp. With it, you grep one line. Always ask the customer for the Event ID before retrying anything.

questions.prompt: Click the menu item that returns you from an impersonated session back to your admin session.
questions.imageAlt: ApisCP top-right dropdown with items: Settings, Help Center, App Index, Revert Login-As, Logout.
questions.explanation: Revert Login-As is the fourth item in the dropdown. The presence of this item is also the tell that you're currently impersonating an account.
